paragraphs.theory: A clear financial goal answers how much you need, by when, and why. Vague wishes rarely become plans; specific targets let you break savings into monthly steps you can track.

question.prompt: Which goal is written in a way that is easiest to act on?

options.text: “Save more money someday.”
“Save $1,200 for a laptop in 12 months ($100/month).”
“Be rich.”
“Spend less on fun.”

paragraphs.theory: Paying yourself first means moving money to savings or investments as soon as income arrives, before discretionary spending. It treats future you as a bill that must be paid.

options.text: Paying all bills before eating out.
Taking a salary from your own business only.
Setting aside savings automatically before optional spending.
Buying things you want on the first day of the month.

paragraphs.theory: Opportunity cost is what you give up when you choose one use for money over another. Naming the trade-off (e.g., vacation vs. emergency fund) makes decisions more honest.

question.prompt: If you spend $500 on a gadget instead of adding it to savings, the opportunity cost is best described as:

options.text: The price of the gadget only.
The next-best thing you could have done with that $500.
Only the sales tax on the purchase.
Whatever your friend spent last week.

paragraphs.theory: A budget is a plan for income and expenses over a period—not a punishment. It shows whether money covers needs, then wants, then goals, so you can adjust before problems grow.

question.prompt: The main purpose of a budget is to:

options.text: Eliminate all fun spending forever.
Plan where money should go and spot gaps early.
Guarantee a raise at work.
Replace a bank account.

paragraphs.theory: Fixed costs (rent, subscriptions) change slowly; variable costs (food out, hobbies) swing month to month. Tracking both helps you see where small leaks add up.

question.prompt: Which is usually a *variable* expense for most people?

options.text: Monthly rent under a 12-month lease.
Groceries and dining out totals that change each month.
A fixed car loan payment.
Annual property tax billed the same each year.

paragraphs.theory: The 50/30/20 rule is a simple split: about 50% needs, 30% wants, 20% savings/debt extra. It is a starting guide—your real percentages depend on income and city cost of living.

question.prompt: In the common 50/30/20 guideline, the “20” is meant for:

options.text: Only entertainment and vacations.
Savings and extra debt payments beyond minimums.
Housing and utilities.
Taxes only.

paragraphs.theory: An emergency fund is cash set aside for job loss, medical bills, or urgent repairs—not planned trips. It reduces the need to borrow at high cost when shocks hit.

question.prompt: Which expense is the *best* fit for an emergency fund?

options.text: A planned annual gym membership renewal.
Sudden job loss covering essential bills for a few months.
A Black Friday TV upgrade.
A birthday gift you knew was coming.

paragraphs.theory: High-yield savings accounts often pay more interest than basic accounts while keeping FDIC insurance limits in mind. Rates move with the economy—compare, don’t set and forget forever.

question.prompt: Compared to keeping emergency cash under a mattress, an insured savings account mainly adds:

options.text: Guaranteed stock market returns.
Safety within insurance limits and some interest.
Tax-free investment gains always.
Unlimited spending rewards.

paragraphs.theory: Automating transfers on payday turns saving into a default behavior. Even small recurring amounts compound into habits and balances over time.

options.text: So you never need to check your balance.
So saving happens before you spend what is left.
To avoid paying taxes.
To lock money forever with no withdrawals.

paragraphs.theory: APR is the yearly cost of borrowing as a percentage, including interest and some fees. Higher APR means more cost for the same balance if you carry debt month to month.

question.prompt: All else equal, which credit card balance costs you more in interest over time?

options.text: The same balance at 18% APR.
The same balance at 28% APR.
They cost the same regardless of APR.
The lower APR always costs more.

paragraphs.theory: Paying only the minimum on cards lengthens payoff time and total interest. Extra payments toward the principal shrink debt faster, especially on high-APR lines.

question.prompt: Making more than the minimum payment on a credit card mainly helps by:

options.text: Raising your APR automatically.
Reducing principal faster and total interest paid.
Removing the need to ever budget.
Guaranteeing a higher credit limit next month.

paragraphs.theory: Credit scores reflect how reliably you repay debt; on-time payments and low utilization help. Scores are tools lenders use—not your worth as a person.

question.prompt: Which behavior usually *helps* a credit score over time?

options.text: Missing payments occasionally.
Keeping card balances low relative to limits and paying on time.
Closing every old card immediately.
Maxing out cards each month.

paragraphs.theory: Compound growth means returns can earn their own returns over long horizons. Time in the market and consistency matter more than timing perfect days for most long-term investors.

question.prompt: Compound interest/growth is best described as:

options.text: Earning returns only on your original deposit, forever.
Earning returns on prior gains as well as principal, over time.
A government guarantee of profit.
The same as simple interest in every case.

paragraphs.theory: Diversification spreads money across many assets so one bad bet does not sink the whole plan. Index funds are one simple way to diversify widely at low cost.

options.text: Eliminate all investment risk.
Reduce the impact of any single holding’s bad outcome.
Guarantee beating every professional trader.
Avoid paying any taxes.

paragraphs.theory: Risk and return tend to go together: higher potential return usually means accepting more volatility. Your timeline and sleep-at-night comfort should guide how much risk you take.

question.prompt: For money you need in full within two years, which stance is generally most cautious?

options.text: Put it all in volatile single stocks seeking max return.
Favor stability and liquidity (e.g., savings or short-term instruments) over chasing high volatility.
Borrow to invest more.
Ignore the timeline and pick random assets.
